4	
5	# Create custom subagents
6	
7	> Create and use specialized AI subagents in Claude Code for task-specific workflows and improved context management.
8	
9	Subagents are specialized AI assistants that handle specific types of tasks. Use one when a side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again: the subagent does that work in its own context and returns only the summary. Define a custom subagent when you keep spawning the same kind of worker with the same instructions.
10	
11	Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent's description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results. To see the context savings in practice, the [context window visualization](/en/context-window) walks through a session where a subagent handles research in its own separate window.
12	
13	<Note>
14	  Subagents work within a single session. To run many independent sessions in parallel and monitor them from one place, see [background agents](/en/agent-view). For sessions that communicate with each other, see [agent teams](/en/agent-teams).
15	</Note>
16	
17	Subagents help you:
18	
19	* **Preserve context** by keeping exploration and implementation out of your main conversation
20	* **Enforce constraints** by limiting which tools a subagent can use
21	* **Reuse configurations** across projects with user-level subagents
22	* **Specialize behavior** with focused system prompts for specific domains
23	* **Control costs** by routing tasks to faster, cheaper models like Haiku
24	
25	Claude uses each subagent's description to decide when to delegate tasks. When you create a subagent, write a clear description so Claude knows when to use it.
26	
27	Claude Code includes several built-in subagents like **Explore**, **Plan**, and **general-purpose**. You can also create custom subagents to handle specific tasks. This page covers:
28	
29	* [Built-in subagents](#built-in-subagents)
30	* [How to create your own](#quickstart-create-your-first-subagent)
31	* [Full configuration options](#configure-subagents)
32	* [Patterns for working with subagents](#work-with-subagents)
33	* [Forked subagents](#fork-the-current-conversation)
34	* [Example subagents](#example-subagents)
35	
36	## Built-in subagents
37	
38	Claude Code includes built-in subagents that Claude automatically uses when appropriate. Each inherits the parent conversation's permissions with additional tool restrictions.
39	
40	Explore and Plan skip your CLAUDE.md files and the parent session's git status to keep research fast and inexpensive. Every other built-in and [custom subagent](#configure-subagents) loads both. For the full breakdown of what reaches a subagent, see [what loads at startup](#what-loads-at-startup).
41	
42	<Tabs>
43	  <Tab title="Explore">
44	    A fast, read-only agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases.
45	
46	    * **Model**: Haiku (fast, low-latency)
47	    * **Tools**: Read-only tools (denied access to Write and Edit tools)
48	    * **Purpose**: File discovery, code search, codebase exploration
49	
50	    Claude delegates to Explore when it needs to search or understand a codebase without making changes. This keeps exploration results out of your main conversation context.
51	
52	    When invoking Explore, Claude specifies a thoroughness level: **quick** for targeted lookups, **medium** for balanced exploration, or **very thorough** for comprehensive analysis.
53	  </Tab>
54	
55	  <Tab title="Plan">
56	    A research agent used during [plan mode](/en/permission-modes#analyze-before-you-edit-with-plan-mode) to gather context before presenting a plan.
57	
58	    * **Model**: Inherits from main conversation
59	    * **Tools**: Read-only tools (denied access to Write and Edit tools)
60	    * **Purpose**: Codebase research for planning
61	
62	    When you're in plan mode and Claude needs to understand your codebase, it delegates research to the Plan subagent. This prevents infinite nesting (subagents cannot spawn other subagents) while still gathering necessary context.
63	  </Tab>
64	
65	  <Tab title="General-purpose">
66	    A capable agent for complex, multi-step tasks that require both exploration and action.
67	
68	    * **Model**: Inherits from main conversation
69	    * **Tools**: All tools
70	    * **Purpose**: Complex research, multi-step operations, code modifications
71	
72	    Claude delegates to general-purpose when the task requires both exploration and modification, complex reasoning to interpret results, or multiple dependent steps.
73	  </Tab>
74	
75	  <Tab title="Other">
76	    Claude Code includes additional helper agents for specific tasks. These are typically invoked automatically, so you don't need to use them directly.
77	
78	    | Agent             | Model  | When Claude uses it                                      |
79	    | :---------------- | :----- | :------------------------------------------------------- |
80	    | statusline-setup  | Sonnet | When you run `/statusline` to configure your status line |
81	    | claude-code-guide | Haiku  | When you ask questions about Claude Code features        |
82	  </Tab>
83	</Tabs>
84	
85	Beyond these built-in subagents, you can create your own with custom prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills. The following sections show how to get started and customize subagents.
86	
87	## Quickstart: create your first subagent
88	
89	Subagents are defined in Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. You can [create them manually](#write-subagent-files) or use the `/agents` command.
90	
91	This walkthrough guides you through creating a user-level subagent with the `/agents` command. The subagent reviews code and suggests improvements for the codebase.
92	
93	<Steps>
94	  <Step title="Open the subagents interface">
95	    In Claude Code, run:
96	
97	    ```text theme={null}
98	    /agents
99	    ```
100	  </Step>
101	
102	  <Step title="Choose a location">
103	    Switch to the **Library** tab, select **Create new agent**, then choose **Personal**. This saves the subagent to `~/.claude/agents/` so it's available in all your projects.
104	  </Step>
105	
106	  <Step title="Generate with Claude">
107	    Select **Generate with Claude**. When prompted, describe the subagent:
108	
109	    ```text theme={null}
110	    A code improvement agent that scans files and suggests improvements
111	    for readability, performance, and best practices. It should explain
112	    each issue, show the current code, and provide an improved version.
113	    ```
114	
115	    Claude generates the identifier, description, and system prompt for you.
116	  </Step>
117	
118	  <Step title="Select tools">
119	    For a read-only reviewer, deselect everything except **Read-only tools**. If you keep all tools selected, the subagent inherits all tools available to the main conversation.
120	  </Step>
121	
122	  <Step title="Select model">
123	    Choose which model the subagent uses. For this example agent, select **Sonnet**, which balances capability and speed for analyzing code patterns.
124	  </Step>
125	
126	  <Step title="Choose a color">
127	    Pick a background color for the subagent. This helps you identify which subagent is running in the UI.
128	  </Step>
129	
130	  <Step title="Configure memory">
131	    Select **User scope** to give the subagent a [persistent memory directory](#enable-persistent-memory) at `~/.claude/agent-memory/`. The subagent uses this to accumulate insights across conversations, such as codebase patterns and recurring issues. Select **None** if you don't want the subagent to persist learnings.
132	  </Step>
133	
134	  <Step title="Save and try it out">
135	    Review the configuration summary. Press `s` or `Enter` to save, or press `e` to save and edit the file in your editor. The subagent is available immediately. Try it:
136	
137	    ```text theme={null}
138	    Use the code-improver agent to suggest improvements in this project
139	    ```
140	
141	    Claude delegates to your new subagent, which scans the codebase and returns improvement suggestions.
142	  </Step>
143	</Steps>
144	
145	You now have a subagent you can use in any project on your machine to analyze codebases and suggest improvements.
146	
147	You can also create subagents manually as Markdown files, define them via CLI flags, or distribute them through plugins. The following sections cover all configuration options.
148	
149	## Configure subagents
150	
151	### Use the /agents command
152	
153	The `/agents` command opens a tabbed interface for managing subagents. The **Running** tab shows live subagents and lets you open or stop them. The **Library** tab lets you:
154	
155	* View all available subagents (built-in, user, project, and plugin)
156	* Create new subagents with guided setup or Claude generation
157	* Edit existing subagent configuration and tool access
158	* Delete custom subagents
159	* See which subagents are active when duplicates exist
160	
161	This is the recommended way to create and manage subagents. For manual creation or automation, you can also add subagent files directly.
162	
163	### Choose the subagent scope
164	
165	Subagents are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. Store them in different locations depending on scope. When multiple subagents share the same name, the higher-priority location wins.
166	
167	| Location                     | Scope                   | Priority    | How to create                                 |
168	| :--------------------------- | :---------------------- | :---------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
169	| Managed settings             | Organization-wide       | 1 (highest) | Deployed via [managed settings](/en/settings) |
170	| `--agents` CLI flag          | Current session         | 2           | Pass JSON when launching Claude Code          |
171	| `.claude/agents/`            | Current project         | 3           | Interactive or manual                         |
172	| `~/.claude/agents/`          | All your projects       | 4           | Interactive or manual                         |
173	| Plugin's `agents/` directory | Where plugin is enabled | 5 (lowest)  | Installed with [plugins](/en/plugins)         |
174	
175	**Project subagents** (`.claude/agents/`) are ideal for subagents specific to a codebase. Check them into version control so your team can use and improve them collaboratively.
176	
177	Project subagents are discovered by walking up from the current working directory. Directories added with `--add-dir` [grant file access only](/en/permissions#additional-directories-grant-file-access-not-configuration) and are not scanned for subagents. To share subagents across projects, use `~/.claude/agents/` or a [plugin](/en/plugins).
178	
179	**User subagents** (`~/.claude/agents/`) are personal subagents available in all your projects.
180	
181	Claude Code scans `.claude/agents/` and `~/.claude/agents/` recursively, so you can organize definitions into subfolders such as `agents/review/` or `agents/research/`. The subdirectory path does not affect how a subagent is identified or invoked, because identity comes only from the `name` frontmatter field. Keep `name` values unique across the whole tree: if two files within one scope declare the same name, Claude Code keeps one and discards the other without warning.
182	
183	Plugin `agents/` directories are also scanned recursively. Unlike project and user scopes, a subfolder inside a plugin's `agents/` directory becomes part of the [scoped identifier](#invoke-subagents-explicitly): a file at `agents/review/security.md` in plugin `my-plugin` registers as `my-plugin:review:security`.
184	
185	**CLI-defined subagents** are passed as JSON when launching Claude Code. They exist only for that session and aren't saved to disk, making them useful for quick testing or automation scripts. You can define multiple subagents in a single `--agents` call:
186	
187	<Tabs>
188	  <Tab title="macOS, Linux, WSL">
189	    ```bash theme={null}
190	    claude --agents '{
191	      "code-reviewer": {
192	        "description": "Expert code reviewer. Use proactively after code changes.",
193	        "prompt": "You are a senior code reviewer. Focus on code quality, security, and best practices.",
194	        "tools": ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash"],
195	        "model": "sonnet"
196	      },
197	      "debugger": {
198	        "description": "Debugging specialist for errors and test failures.",
199	        "prompt": "You are an expert debugger. Analyze errors, identify root causes, and provide fixes."
200	      }
201	    }'
202	    ```
203	  </Tab>
204	
205	  <Tab title="Windows PowerShell">
206	    ```powershell theme={null}
207	    claude --agents @'
208	    {
209	      "code-reviewer": {
210	        "description": "Expert code reviewer. Use proactively after code changes.",
211	        "prompt": "You are a senior code reviewer. Focus on code quality, security, and best practices.",
212	        "tools": ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash"],
213	        "model": "sonnet"
214	      },
215	      "debugger": {
216	        "description": "Debugging specialist for errors and test failures.",
217	        "prompt": "You are an expert debugger. Analyze errors, identify root causes, and provide fixes."
218	      }
219	    }
220	    '@
221	    ```
222	  </Tab>
223	</Tabs>
224	
225	The `--agents` flag accepts JSON with the same [frontmatter](#supported-frontmatter-fields) fields as file-based subagents: `description`, `prompt`, `tools`, `disallowedTools`, `model`, `permissionMode`, `mcpServers`, `hooks`, `maxTurns`, `skills`, `initialPrompt`, `memory`, `effort`, `background`, `isolation`, and `color`. Use `prompt` for the system prompt, equivalent to the markdown body in file-based subagents.
226	
227	**Managed subagents** are deployed by organization administrators. Place markdown files in `.claude/agents/` inside the [managed settings directory](/en/settings#settings-files), using the same frontmatter format as project and user subagents. Managed definitions take precedence over project and user subagents with the same name.
228	
229	**Plugin subagents** come from [plugins](/en/plugins) you've installed. They appear in `/agents` alongside your custom subagents. See the [plugin components reference](/en/plugins-reference#agents) for details on creating plugin subagents.
230	
231	<Note>
232	  For security reasons, plugin subagents do not support the `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter fields. These fields are ignored when loading agents from a plugin. If you need them, copy the agent file into `.claude/agents/` or `~/.claude/agents/`. You can also add rules to [`permissions.allow`](/en/settings#permission-settings) in `settings.json` or `settings.local.json`, but these rules apply to the entire session, not just the plugin subagent.
233	</Note>
234	
235	Subagent definitions from any of these scopes are also available to [agent teams](/en/agent-teams#use-subagent-definitions-for-teammates): when spawning a teammate, you can reference a subagent type and the teammate uses its `tools` and `model`, with the definition's body appended to the teammate's system prompt as additional instructions. See [agent teams](/en/agent-teams#use-subagent-definitions-for-teammates) for which frontmatter fields apply on that path.
236	
237	### Write subagent files
238	
239	Subagent files use YAML frontmatter for configuration, followed by the system prompt in Markdown:
240	
241	<Note>
242	  Subagents are loaded at session start. If you add or edit a subagent file directly on disk, restart your session to load it. Subagents created through the `/agents` interface take effect immediately without a restart.
243	</Note>
244	
245	```markdown theme={null}
246	---
247	name: code-reviewer
248	description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
249	tools: Read, Glob, Grep
250	model: sonnet
251	---
252	
253	You are a code reviewer. When invoked, analyze the code and provide
254	specific, actionable feedback on quality, security, and best practices.
255	```
256	
257	The frontmatter defines the subagent's metadata and configuration. The body becomes the system prompt that guides the subagent's behavior. Subagents receive only this system prompt (plus basic environment details like working directory), not the full Claude Code system prompt.
258	
259	A subagent starts in the main conversation's current working directory. Within a subagent, `cd` commands do not persist between Bash or PowerShell tool calls and do not affect the main conversation's working directory. To give the subagent an isolated copy of the repository instead, set [`isolation: worktree`](#supported-frontmatter-fields).
260	
261	#### Supported frontmatter fields
262	
263	The following fields can be used in the YAML frontmatter. Only `name` and `description` are required.
264	
265	| Field             | Required | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
266	| :---------------- | :------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
267	| `name`            | Yes      | Unique identifier using lowercase letters and hyphens. [Hooks](/en/hooks#subagentstart) receive this value as `agent_type`. The filename does not have to match                                                                                                                                                                          |
268	| `description`     | Yes      | When Claude should delegate to this subagent                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
269	| `tools`           | No       | [Tools](#available-tools) the subagent can use. Inherits all tools if omitted. To preload Skills into context, use the `skills` field rather than listing `Skill` here                                                                                                                                                                   |
270	| `disallowedTools` | No       | Tools to deny, removed from inherited or specified list                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
271	| `model`           | No       | [Model](#choose-a-model) to use: `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, a full model ID (for example, `claude-opus-4-7`), or `inherit`. Defaults to `inherit`                                                                                                                                                                                        |
272	| `permissionMode`  | No       | [Permission mode](#permission-modes): `default`, `acceptEdits`, `auto`, `dontAsk`, `bypassPermissions`, or `plan`. Ignored for [plugin subagents](#choose-the-subagent-scope)                                                                                                                                                            |
273	| `maxTurns`        | No       | Maximum number of agentic turns before the subagent stops                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
274	| `skills`          | No       | [Skills](/en/skills) to preload into the subagent's context at startup. The full skill content is injected, not just the description. Subagents can still invoke unlisted project, user, and plugin skills through the Skill tool                                                                                                        |
275	| `mcpServers`      | No       | [MCP servers](/en/mcp) available to this subagent. Each entry is either a server name referencing an already-configured server (e.g., `"slack"`) or an inline definition with the server name as key and a full [MCP server config](/en/mcp#installing-mcp-servers) as value. Ignored for [plugin subagents](#choose-the-subagent-scope) |
276	| `hooks`           | No       | [Lifecycle hooks](#define-hooks-for-subagents) scoped to this subagent. Ignored for [plugin subagents](#choose-the-subagent-scope)                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
277	| `memory`          | No       | [Persistent memory scope](#enable-persistent-memory): `user`, `project`, or `local`. Enables cross-session learning                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
278	| `background`      | No       | Set to `true` to always run this subagent as a [background task](#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background). Default: `false`                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
279	| `effort`          | No       | Effort level when this subagent is active. Overrides the session effort level. Default: inherits from session. Options: `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`, `max`; available levels depend on the model                                                                                                                                    |
280	| `isolation`       | No       | Set to `worktree` to run the subagent in a temporary [git worktree](/en/worktrees), giving it an isolated copy of the repository. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes                                                                                                                              |
281	| `color`           | No       | Display color for the subagent in the task list and transcript. Accepts `red`, `blue`, `green`, `yellow`, `purple`, `orange`, `pink`, or `cyan`                                                                                                                                                                                          |
282	| `initialPrompt`   | No       | Auto-submitted as the first user turn when this agent runs as the main session agent (via `--agent` or the `agent` setting). [Commands](/en/commands) and [skills](/en/skills) are processed. Prepended to any user-provided prompt                                                                                                      |
283	
284	### Choose a model
285	
286	The `model` field controls which [AI model](/en/model-config) the subagent uses:
287	
288	* **Model alias**: Use one of the available aliases: `sonnet`, `opus`, or `haiku`
289	* **Full model ID**: Use a full model ID such as `claude-opus-4-7` or `claude-sonnet-4-6`. Accepts the same values as the `--model` flag
290	* **inherit**: Use the same model as the main conversation
291	* **Omitted**: If not specified, defaults to `inherit` (uses the same model as the main conversation)
292	
293	When Claude invokes a subagent, it can also pass a `model` parameter for that specific invocation. Claude Code resolves the subagent's model in this order:
294	
295	1. The [`CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL`](/en/model-config#environment-variables) environment variable, if set
296	2. The per-invocation `model` parameter
297	3. The subagent definition's `model` frontmatter
298	4. The main conversation's model
299	
300	### Control subagent capabilities
301	
302	You can control what subagents can do through tool access, permission modes, and conditional rules.
303	
304	#### Available tools
305	
306	Subagents can use any of Claude Code's [internal tools](/en/tools-reference). By default, subagents inherit all tools from the main conversation, including MCP tools.
307	
308	To restrict tools, use either the `tools` field (allowlist) or the `disallowedTools` field (denylist). This example uses `tools` to exclusively allow Read, Grep, Glob, and Bash. The subagent can't edit files, write files, or use any MCP tools:
309	
310	```yaml theme={null}
311	---
312	name: safe-researcher
313	description: Research agent with restricted capabilities
314	tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
315	---
316	```
317	
318	This example uses `disallowedTools` to inherit every tool from the main conversation except Write and Edit. The subagent keeps Bash, MCP tools, and everything else:
319	
320	```yaml theme={null}
321	---
322	name: no-writes
323	description: Inherits every tool except file writes
324	disallowedTools: Write, Edit
325	---
326	```
327	
328	If both are set, `disallowedTools` is applied first, then `tools` is resolved against the remaining pool. A tool listed in both is removed.
329	
330	#### Restrict which subagents can be spawned
331	
332	When an agent runs as the main thread with `claude --agent`, it can spawn subagents using the Agent tool. To restrict which subagent types it can spawn, use `Agent(agent_type)` syntax in the `tools` field.
333	
334	<Note>In version 2.1.63, the Task tool was renamed to Agent. Existing `Task(...)` references in settings and agent definitions still work as aliases.</Note>
335	
336	```yaml theme={null}
337	---
338	name: coordinator
339	description: Coordinates work across specialized agents
340	tools: Agent(worker, researcher), Read, Bash
341	---
342	```
343	
344	This is an allowlist: only the `worker` and `researcher` subagents can be spawned. If the agent tries to spawn any other type, the request fails and the agent sees only the allowed types in its prompt. To block specific agents while allowing all others, use [`permissions.deny`](#disable-specific-subagents) instead.
345	
346	To allow spawning any subagent without restrictions, use `Agent` without parentheses:
347	
348	```yaml theme={null}
349	tools: Agent, Read, Bash
350	```
351	
352	If `Agent` is omitted from the `tools` list entirely, the agent cannot spawn any subagents. This restriction only applies to agents running as the main thread with `claude --agent`. Subagents cannot spawn other subagents, so `Agent(agent_type)` has no effect in subagent definitions.
353	
354	#### Scope MCP servers to a subagent
355	
356	Use the `mcpServers` field to give a subagent access to [MCP](/en/mcp) servers that aren't available in the main conversation. Inline servers defined here are connected when the subagent starts and disconnected when it finishes. String references share the parent session's connection.
357	
358	<Note>
359	  The `mcpServers` field applies in both contexts where an agent file can run:
360	
361	  * As a subagent, spawned through the Agent tool or an @-mention
362	  * As the main session, launched with [`--agent`](#invoke-subagents-explicitly) or the `agent` setting
363	
364	  When the agent is the main session, inline server definitions connect at startup alongside servers from [`.mcp.json`](/en/mcp) and settings files.
365	</Note>
366	
367	Each entry in the list is either an inline server definition or a string referencing an MCP server already configured in your session:
368	
369	```yaml theme={null}
370	---
371	name: browser-tester
372	description: Tests features in a real browser using Playwright
373	mcpServers:
374	  # Inline definition: scoped to this subagent only
375	  - playwright:
376	      type: stdio
377	      command: npx
378	      args: ["-y", "@playwright/mcp@latest"]
379	  # Reference by name: reuses an already-configured server
380	  - github
381	---
382	
383	Use the Playwright tools to navigate, screenshot, and interact with pages.
384	```
385	
386	Inline definitions use the same schema as `.mcp.json` server entries (`stdio`, `http`, `sse`, `ws`), keyed by the server name.
387	
388	To keep an MCP server out of the main conversation entirely and avoid its tool descriptions consuming context there, define it inline here rather than in `.mcp.json`. The subagent gets the tools; the parent conversation does not.
389	
390	#### Permission modes
391	
392	The `permissionMode` field controls how the subagent handles permission prompts. Subagents inherit the permission context from the main conversation and can override the mode, except when the parent mode takes precedence as described below.
393	
394	| Mode                | Behavior                                                                                                                                    |
395	| :------------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
396	| `default`           | Standard permission checking with prompts                                                                                                   |
397	| `acceptEdits`       | Auto-accept file edits and common filesystem commands for paths in the working directory or `additionalDirectories`                         |
398	| `auto`              | [Auto mode](/en/permission-modes#eliminate-prompts-with-auto-mode): a background classifier reviews commands and protected-directory writes |
399	| `dontAsk`           | Auto-deny permission prompts (explicitly allowed tools still work)                                                                          |
400	| `bypassPermissions` | Skip permission prompts                                                                                                                     |
401	| `plan`              | Plan mode (read-only exploration)                                                                                                           |
402	
403	<Warning>
404	  Use `bypassPermissions` with caution. It skips all permission prompts, allowing the subagent to execute operations without approval, including writes to `.git`, `.claude`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, and `.husky`. Root and home directory removals such as `rm -rf /` still prompt as a circuit breaker. See [permission modes](/en/permission-modes#skip-all-checks-with-bypasspermissions-mode) for details.
405	</Warning>
406	
407	If the parent uses `bypassPermissions` or `acceptEdits`, this takes precedence and cannot be overridden. If the parent uses [auto mode](/en/permission-modes#eliminate-prompts-with-auto-mode), the subagent inherits auto mode and any `permissionMode` in its frontmatter is ignored: the classifier evaluates the subagent's tool calls with the same block and allow rules as the parent session.
408	
409	#### Preload skills into subagents
410	
411	Use the `skills` field to inject skill content into a subagent's context at startup. This gives the subagent domain knowledge without requiring it to discover and load skills during execution.
412	
413	```yaml theme={null}
414	---
415	name: api-developer
416	description: Implement API endpoints following team conventions
417	skills:
418	  - api-conventions
419	  - error-handling-patterns
420	---
421	
422	Implement API endpoints. Follow the conventions and patterns from the preloaded skills.
423	```
424	
425	The full content of each listed skill is injected into the subagent's context at startup. This field controls which skills are preloaded, not which skills the subagent can access: without it, the subagent can still discover and invoke project, user, and plugin skills through the Skill tool during execution. To prevent a subagent from invoking skills entirely, omit `Skill` from the [`tools`](#available-tools) list or add it to `disallowedTools`.
426	
427	You cannot preload skills that set [`disable-model-invocation: true`](/en/skills#control-who-invokes-a-skill), since preloading draws from the same set of skills Claude can invoke. If a listed skill is missing or disabled, Claude Code skips it and logs a warning to the debug log.
428	
429	<Note>
430	  This is the inverse of [running a skill in a subagent](/en/skills#run-skills-in-a-subagent). With `skills` in a subagent, the subagent controls the system prompt and loads skill content. With `context: fork` in a skill, the skill content is injected into the agent you specify. Both use the same underlying system.
431	</Note>
432	
433	#### Enable persistent memory
434	
435	The `memory` field gives the subagent a persistent directory that survives across conversations. The subagent uses this directory to build up knowledge over time, such as codebase patterns, debugging insights, and architectural decisions.
436	
437	```yaml theme={null}
438	---
439	name: code-reviewer
440	description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
441	memory: user
442	---
443	
444	You are a code reviewer. As you review code, update your agent memory with
445	patterns, conventions, and recurring issues you discover.
446	```
447	
448	Choose a scope based on how broadly the memory should apply:
449	
450	| Scope     | Location                                      | Use when                                                                                    |
451	| :-------- | :-------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
452	| `user`    | `~/.claude/agent-memory/<name-of-agent>/`     | the subagent should remember learnings across all projects                                  |
453	| `project` | `.claude/agent-memory/<name-of-agent>/`       | the subagent's knowledge is project-specific and shareable via version control              |
454	| `local`   | `.claude/agent-memory-local/<name-of-agent>/` | the subagent's knowledge is project-specific but should not be checked into version control |
455	
456	When memory is enabled:
457	
458	* The subagent's system prompt includes instructions for reading and writing to the memory directory.
459	* The subagent's system prompt also includes the first 200 lines or 25KB of `MEMORY.md` in the memory directory, whichever comes first, with instructions to curate `MEMORY.md` if it exceeds that limit.
460	* Read, Write, and Edit tools are automatically enabled so the subagent can manage its memory files.
461	
462	##### Persistent memory tips
463	
464	* `project` is the recommended default scope. It makes subagent knowledge shareable via version control. Use `user` when the subagent's knowledge is broadly applicable across projects, or `local` when the knowledge should not be checked into version control.
465	* Ask the subagent to consult its memory before starting work: "Review this PR, and check your memory for patterns you've seen before."
466	* Ask the subagent to update its memory after completing a task: "Now that you're done, save what you learned to your memory." Over time, this builds a knowledge base that makes the subagent more effective.
467	* Include memory instructions directly in the subagent's markdown file so it proactively maintains its own knowledge base:
468	
469	  ```markdown theme={null}
470	  Update your agent memory as you discover codepaths, patterns, library
471	  locations, and key architectural decisions. This builds up institutional
472	  knowledge across conversations. Write concise notes about what you found
473	  and where.
474	  ```
475	
476	#### Conditional rules with hooks
477	
478	For more dynamic control over tool usage, use `PreToolUse` hooks to validate operations before they execute. This is useful when you need to allow some operations of a tool while blocking others.
479	
480	This example creates a subagent that only allows read-only database queries. The `PreToolUse` hook runs the script specified in `command` before each Bash command executes:
481	
482	```yaml theme={null}
483	---
484	name: db-reader
485	description: Execute read-only database queries
486	tools: Bash
487	hooks:
488	  PreToolUse:
489	    - matcher: "Bash"
490	      hooks:
491	        - type: command
492	          command: "./scripts/validate-readonly-query.sh"
493	---
494	```
495	
496	Claude Code [passes hook input as JSON](/en/hooks#pretooluse-input) via stdin to hook commands. The validation script reads this JSON, extracts the Bash command, and [exits with code 2](/en/hooks#exit-code-2-behavior-per-event) to block write operations:
497	
498	```bash theme={null}
499	#!/bin/bash
500	# ./scripts/validate-readonly-query.sh
501	
502	INPUT=$(cat)
503	COMMAND=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
504	
505	# Block SQL write operations (case-insensitive)
506	if echo "$COMMAND" | grep -iE '\b(INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP|CREATE|ALTER|TRUNCATE)\b' > /dev/null; then
507	  echo "Blocked: Only SELECT queries are allowed" >&2
508	  exit 2
509	fi
510	
511	exit 0
512	```
513	
514	See [Hook input](/en/hooks#pretooluse-input) for the complete input schema and [exit codes](/en/hooks#exit-code-output) for how exit codes affect behavior. On Windows, write hook scripts in PowerShell and add `shell: powershell` to the hook entry as shown in [running hooks in PowerShell](/en/hooks#windows-powershell-tool).
515	
516	#### Disable specific subagents
517	
518	You can prevent Claude from using specific subagents by adding them to the `deny` array in your [settings](/en/settings#permission-settings). Use the format `Agent(subagent-name)` where `subagent-name` matches the subagent's name field.
519	
520	```json theme={null}
521	{
522	  "permissions": {
523	    "deny": ["Agent(Explore)", "Agent(my-custom-agent)"]
524	  }
525	}
526	```
527	
528	This works for both built-in and custom subagents. You can also use the `--disallowedTools` CLI flag:
529	
530	```bash theme={null}
531	claude --disallowedTools "Agent(Explore)"
532	```
533	
534	See [Permissions documentation](/en/permissions#tool-specific-permission-rules) for more details on permission rules.
535	
536	### Define hooks for subagents
537	
538	Subagents can define [hooks](/en/hooks) that run during the subagent's lifecycle. There are two ways to configure hooks:
539	
540	1. **In the subagent's frontmatter**: Define hooks that run only while that subagent is active
541	2. **In `settings.json`**: Define hooks that run in the main session when subagents start or stop
542	
543	#### Hooks in subagent frontmatter
544	
545	Define hooks directly in the subagent's markdown file. These hooks only run while that specific subagent is active and are cleaned up when it finishes.
546	
547	<Note>
548	  Frontmatter hooks fire when the agent is spawned as a subagent through the Agent tool or an @-mention, and when the agent runs as the main session via [`--agent`](#invoke-subagents-explicitly) or the `agent` setting. In the main-session case they run alongside any hooks defined in [`settings.json`](/en/hooks).
549	</Note>
550	
551	All [hook events](/en/hooks#hook-events) are supported. The most common events for subagents are:
552	
553	| Event         | Matcher input | When it fires                                                       |
554	| :------------ | :------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------ |
555	| `PreToolUse`  | Tool name     | Before the subagent uses a tool                                     |
556	| `PostToolUse` | Tool name     | After the subagent uses a tool                                      |
557	| `Stop`        | (none)        | When the subagent finishes (converted to `SubagentStop` at runtime) |
558	
559	This example validates Bash commands with the `PreToolUse` hook and runs a linter after file edits with `PostToolUse`:
560	
561	```yaml theme={null}
562	---
563	name: code-reviewer
564	description: Review code changes with automatic linting
565	hooks:
566	  PreToolUse:
567	    - matcher: "Bash"
568	      hooks:
569	        - type: command
570	          command: "./scripts/validate-command.sh $TOOL_INPUT"
571	  PostToolUse:
572	    - matcher: "Edit|Write"
573	      hooks:
574	        - type: command
575	          command: "./scripts/run-linter.sh"
576	---
577	```
578	
579	When the agent is invoked as a subagent, `Stop` hooks in frontmatter are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` events.
580	
581	#### Project-level hooks for subagent events
582	
583	Configure hooks in `settings.json` that respond to subagent lifecycle events in the main session.
584	
585	| Event           | Matcher input   | When it fires                    |
586	| :-------------- | :-------------- | :------------------------------- |
587	| `SubagentStart` | Agent type name | When a subagent begins execution |
588	| `SubagentStop`  | Agent type name | When a subagent completes        |
589	
590	Both events support matchers to target specific agent types by name. This example runs a setup script only when the `db-agent` subagent starts, and a cleanup script when any subagent stops:
591	
592	```json theme={null}
593	{
594	  "hooks": {
595	    "SubagentStart": [
596	      {
597	        "matcher": "db-agent",
598	        "hooks": [
599	          { "type": "command", "command": "./scripts/setup-db-connection.sh" }
600	        ]
601	      }
602	    ],
603	    "SubagentStop": [
604	      {
605	        "hooks": [
606	          { "type": "command", "command": "./scripts/cleanup-db-connection.sh" }
607	        ]
608	      }
609	    ]
610	  }
611	}
612	```
613	
614	See [Hooks](/en/hooks) for the complete hook configuration format.
615	
616	## Work with subagents
617	
618	### Understand automatic delegation
619	
620	Claude automatically delegates tasks based on the task description in your request, the `description` field in subagent configurations, and current context. To encourage proactive delegation, include phrases like "use proactively" in your subagent's description field.
621	
622	### Invoke subagents explicitly
623	
624	When automatic delegation isn't enough, you can request a subagent yourself. Three patterns escalate from a one-off suggestion to a session-wide default:
625	
626	* **Natural language**: name the subagent in your prompt; Claude decides whether to delegate
627	* **@-mention**: guarantees the subagent runs for one task
628	* **Session-wide**: the whole session uses that subagent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model via the `--agent` flag or the `agent` setting
629	
630	For natural language, there's no special syntax. Name the subagent and Claude typically delegates:
631	
632	```text theme={null}
633	Use the test-runner subagent to fix failing tests
634	Have the code-reviewer subagent look at my recent changes
635	```
636	
637	**@-mention the subagent.** Type `@` and pick the subagent from the typeahead, the same way you @-mention files. This ensures that specific subagent runs rather than leaving the choice to Claude:
638	
639	```text theme={null}
640	@"code-reviewer (agent)" look at the auth changes
641	```
642	
643	Your full message still goes to Claude, which writes the subagent's task prompt based on what you asked. The @-mention controls which subagent Claude invokes, not what prompt it receives.
644	
645	Subagents provided by an enabled [plugin](/en/plugins) appear in the typeahead under their scoped name, such as `my-plugin:code-reviewer` or `my-plugin:review:security` when the plugin [organizes agents into subfolders](#choose-the-subagent-scope). Named background subagents currently running in the session also appear in the typeahead, showing their status next to the name. You can also type the mention manually without using the picker: `@agent-<name>` for local subagents, or `@agent-` followed by the scoped name for plugin subagents, for example `@agent-my-plugin:code-reviewer`.
646	
647	**Run the whole session as a subagent.** Pass [`--agent <name>`](/en/cli-reference) to start a session where the main thread itself takes on that subagent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model:
648	
649	```bash theme={null}
650	claude --agent code-reviewer
651	```
652	
653	The subagent's system prompt replaces the default Claude Code system prompt entirely, the same way [`--system-prompt`](/en/cli-reference) does. `CLAUDE.md` files and project memory still load through the normal message flow. The agent name appears as `@<name>` in the startup header so you can confirm it's active.
654	
655	This works with built-in and custom subagents, and the choice persists when you resume the session.
656	
657	For a plugin-provided subagent, you can pass just the agent name and Claude Code will find it:
658	
659	```bash theme={null}
660	claude --agent security-reviewer
661	```
662	
663	If multiple plugins provide agents with the same name, pass the scoped name to disambiguate:
664	
665	```bash theme={null}
666	claude --agent my-plugin:security-reviewer
667	```
668	
669	If the plugin places the agent in a subfolder of its `agents/` directory, include the subfolder in the scoped name, for example `claude --agent my-plugin:review:security`.
670	
671	To make it the default for every session in a project, set `agent` in `.claude/settings.json`:
672	
673	```json theme={null}
674	{
675	  "agent": "code-reviewer"
676	}
677	```
678	
679	The CLI flag overrides the setting if both are present.
680	
681	### Run subagents in foreground or background
682	
683	Subagents can run in the foreground (blocking) or background (concurrent):
684	
685	* **Foreground subagents** block the main conversation until complete. Permission prompts are passed through to you as they come up.
686	* **Background subagents** run concurrently while you continue working. They run with the permissions already granted in the session and auto-deny any tool call that would otherwise prompt. If a background subagent needs to ask clarifying questions, that tool call fails but the subagent continues.
687	
688	If a background subagent fails due to missing permissions, you can start a new foreground subagent with the same task to retry with interactive prompts.
689	
690	Claude decides whether to run subagents in the foreground or background based on the task. You can also:
691	
692	* Ask Claude to "run this in the background"
693	* Press **Ctrl+B** to background a running task
694	
695	To disable all background task functionality, set the `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS` environment variable to `1`. See [Environment variables](/en/env-vars).
696	
697	When [fork mode](#fork-the-current-conversation) is enabled, every subagent spawn runs in the background regardless of the `background` field. Forks still surface permission prompts in your terminal as they occur; named subagents auto-deny anything that would prompt, as described above.
698	
699	### Common patterns
700	
701	#### Isolate high-volume operations
702	
703	One of the most effective uses for subagents is isolating operations that produce large amounts of output. Running tests, fetching documentation, or processing log files can consume significant context. By delegating these to a subagent, the verbose output stays in the subagent's context while only the relevant summary returns to your main conversation.
704	
705	```text theme={null}
706	Use a subagent to run the test suite and report only the failing tests with their error messages
707	```
708	
709	#### Run parallel research
710	
711	For independent investigations, spawn multiple subagents to work simultaneously:
712	
713	```text theme={null}
714	Research the authentication, database, and API modules in parallel using separate subagents
715	```
716	
717	Each subagent explores its area independently, then Claude synthesizes the findings. This works best when the research paths don't depend on each other.
718	
719	<Warning>
720	  When subagents complete, their results return to your main conversation. Running many subagents that each return detailed results can consume significant context.
721	</Warning>
722	
723	For tasks that need sustained parallelism or exceed your context window, [agent teams](/en/agent-teams) give each worker its own independent context.
724	
725	#### Chain subagents
726	
727	For multi-step workflows, ask Claude to use subagents in sequence. Each subagent completes its task and returns results to Claude, which then passes relevant context to the next subagent.
728	
729	```text theme={null}
730	Use the code-reviewer subagent to find performance issues, then use the optimizer subagent to fix them
731	```
732	
733	### Choose between subagents and main conversation
734	
735	Use the **main conversation** when:
736	
737	* The task needs frequent back-and-forth or iterative refinement
738	* Multiple phases share significant context (planning → implementation → testing)
739	* You're making a quick, targeted change
740	* Latency matters. Subagents start fresh and may need time to gather context
741	
742	Use **subagents** when:
743	
744	* The task produces verbose output you don't need in your main context
745	* You want to enforce specific tool restrictions or permissions
746	* The work is self-contained and can return a summary
747	
748	Consider [Skills](/en/skills) instead when you want reusable prompts or workflows that run in the main conversation context rather than isolated subagent context.
749	
750	For a quick question about something already in your conversation, use [`/btw`](/en/interactive-mode#side-questions-with-%2Fbtw) instead of a subagent. It sees your full context but has no tool access, and the answer is discarded rather than added to history.
751	
752	<Note>
753	  Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. If your workflow requires nested delegation, use [Skills](/en/skills) or [chain subagents](#chain-subagents) from the main conversation.
754	</Note>
755	
756	### Manage subagent context
757	
758	#### What loads at startup
759	
760	Each subagent starts with a fresh, isolated context window. It does not see your conversation history, the skills you've already invoked, or the files Claude has already read. Claude composes a delegation message that summarizes the task, and the subagent works from there. The exception is a [fork](#fork-the-current-conversation), which inherits the parent conversation instead of starting fresh.
761	
762	A non-fork subagent's initial context contains:
763	
764	* **System prompt**: the agent's own prompt plus environment details that Claude Code appends, not the full Claude Code system prompt. Custom subagents define theirs in the [markdown body](#write-subagent-files) or `prompt` field. Built-in agents have predefined prompts.
765	* **Task message**: the delegation prompt Claude writes when it hands off the work.
766	* **CLAUDE.md and memory**: every level of the [memory hierarchy](/en/memory#how-claude-md-files-load) the main conversation loads, including `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, project rules, `CLAUDE.local.md`, and managed policy files. The built-in Explore and Plan agents skip this.
767	* **Git status**: a snapshot taken at the start of the parent session. Absent when the working directory isn't a Git repository or when [`includeGitInstructions`](/en/settings#available-settings) is `false`. Explore and Plan skip it regardless.
768	* **Preloaded skills**: full content of any skill named in the agent's [`skills` field](#preload-skills-into-subagents). Built-in agents don't preload skills.
769	
770	Explore and Plan are the only subagents that omit CLAUDE.md and git status. There is no frontmatter field or per-agent setting to change which agents skip them.
771	
772	The main conversation reads Explore and Plan results with full CLAUDE.md context, so most rules don't need to reach the subagent itself. If a rule must, such as "ignore the `vendor/` directory," restate it in the prompt you give Claude when delegating.
773	
774	#### Resume subagents
775	
776	Each subagent invocation creates a new instance with fresh context. To continue an existing subagent's work instead of starting over, ask Claude to resume it.
777	
778	Resumed subagents retain their full conversation history, including all previous tool calls, results, and reasoning. The subagent picks up exactly where it stopped rather than starting fresh.
779	
780	When a subagent completes, Claude receives its agent ID. Claude uses the `SendMessage` tool with the agent's ID as the `to` field to resume it. The `SendMessage` tool is only available when [agent teams](/en/agent-teams) are enabled via `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`.
781	
782	To resume a subagent, ask Claude to continue the previous work:
783	
784	```text theme={null}
785	Use the code-reviewer subagent to review the authentication module
786	[Agent completes]
787	
788	Continue that code review and now analyze the authorization logic
789	[Claude resumes the subagent with full context from previous conversation]
790	```
791	
792	If a stopped subagent receives a `SendMessage`, it auto-resumes in the background without requiring a new `Agent` invocation.
793	
794	You can also ask Claude for the agent ID if you want to reference it explicitly, or find IDs in the transcript files at `~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/`. Each transcript is stored as `agent-{agentId}.jsonl`.
795	
796	Subagent transcripts persist independently of the main conversation:
797	
798	* **Main conversation compaction**: When the main conversation compacts, subagent transcripts are unaffected. They're stored in separate files.
799	* **Session persistence**: Subagent transcripts persist within their session. You can [resume a subagent](#resume-subagents) after restarting Claude Code by resuming the same session.
800	* **Automatic cleanup**: Transcripts are cleaned up based on the `cleanupPeriodDays` setting (default: 30 days).
801	
802	#### Auto-compaction
803	
804	Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation. By default, auto-compaction triggers at approximately 95% capacity. To trigger compaction earlier, set `CLAUDE_AUTOCOMPACT_PCT_OVERRIDE` to a lower percentage (for example, `50`). See [environment variables](/en/env-vars) for details.
805	
806	Compaction events are logged in subagent transcript files:
807	
808	```json theme={null}
809	{
810	  "type": "system",
811	  "subtype": "compact_boundary",
812	  "compactMetadata": {
813	    "trigger": "auto",
814	    "preTokens": 167189
815	  }
816	}
817	```
818	
819	The `preTokens` value shows how many tokens were used before compaction occurred.
820	
821	## Fork the current conversation
822	
823	<Note>
824	  Forked subagents are experimental and require Claude Code v2.1.117 or later. Behavior and configuration may change in future releases. Enable them by setting the [`CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT`](/en/env-vars) environment variable to `1`. The variable is honored in interactive mode and via the SDK or `claude -p`.
825	</Note>
826	
827	A fork is a subagent that inherits the entire conversation so far instead of starting fresh. This drops the input isolation that subagents otherwise provide: a fork sees the same system prompt, tools, model, and message history as the main session, so you can hand it a side task without re-explaining the situation. The fork's own tool calls still stay out of your conversation and only its final result comes back, so your main context window stays clean. Use a fork when a named subagent would need too much background to be useful, or when you want to try several approaches in parallel from the same starting point.
828	
829	Enabling fork mode changes Claude Code in three ways:
830	
831	* Claude spawns a fork whenever it would otherwise use the [general-purpose](#built-in-subagents) subagent. Named subagents such as Explore still spawn as before.
832	* Every subagent spawn runs in the [background](#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background), whether it is a fork or a named subagent. Set `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS` to `1` to keep spawns synchronous.
833	* The `/fork` command spawns a fork instead of acting as an alias for [`/branch`](/en/commands).
834	
835	You can start a fork yourself with `/fork` followed by a directive. Claude Code names the fork from the first words of the directive. The following example forks the conversation to draft test cases while you continue with the implementation in the main session:
836	
837	```text theme={null}
838	/fork draft unit tests for the parser changes so far
839	```
840	
841	The fork appears in a panel below your prompt and runs in the background while you keep working. When it finishes, its result arrives as a message in your main conversation. The next section covers the panel controls for watching and steering forks while they run.
842	
843	### Observe and steer running forks
844	
845	Running forks appear in a panel below the prompt input, with one row for the main session and one for each fork. Use these keys to interact with the panel:
846	
847	| Key       | Action                                                             |
848	| :-------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------- |
849	| `↑` / `↓` | Move between rows                                                  |
850	| `Enter`   | Open the selected fork's transcript and send it follow-up messages |
851	| `x`       | Dismiss a finished fork or stop a running one                      |
852	| `Esc`     | Return focus to the prompt input                                   |
853	
854	### How forks differ from named subagents
855	
856	A fork inherits everything the main session has at the moment it spawns. A named subagent starts from its own definition.
857	
858	|                         | Fork                             | Named subagent                                                                           |
859	| :---------------------- | :------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
860	| Context                 | Full conversation history        | Fresh context with the prompt you pass                                                   |
861	| System prompt and tools | Same as main session             | From the subagent's [definition file](#write-subagent-files)                             |
862	| Model                   | Same as main session             | From the subagent's `model` field                                                        |
863	| Permissions             | Prompts surface in your terminal | [Auto-denied](#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background) when running in the background |
864	| Prompt cache            | Shared with main session         | Separate cache                                                                           |
865	
866	Because a fork's system prompt and tool definitions are identical to the parent, its first request reuses the parent's [prompt cache](/en/prompt-caching#subagents-and-the-cache). This makes forking cheaper than spawning a fresh subagent for tasks that need the same context.
867	
868	When Claude spawns a fork through the Agent tool, it can pass `isolation: "worktree"` so the fork's file edits are written to a separate git worktree instead of your checkout.
869	
870	### Limitations
871	
872	Setting `CLAUDE_CODE_FORK_SUBAGENT=1` enables fork mode in interactive sessions, [non-interactive mode](/en/headless), and the Agent SDK. A fork cannot spawn further forks.
873	
874	## Example subagents
875	
876	These examples demonstrate effective patterns for building subagents. Use them as starting points, or generate a customized version with Claude.
877	
878	<Tip>
879	  **Best practices:**
880	
881	  * **Design focused subagents:** each subagent should excel at one specific task
882	  * **Write detailed descriptions:** Claude uses the description to decide when to delegate
883	  * **Limit tool access:** grant only necessary permissions for security and focus
884	  * **Check into version control:** share project subagents with your team
885	</Tip>
886	
887	### Code reviewer
888	
889	A read-only subagent that reviews code without modifying it. This example shows how to design a focused subagent with limited tool access (no Edit or Write) and a detailed prompt that specifies exactly what to look for and how to format output.
890	
891	```markdown theme={null}
892	---
893	name: code-reviewer
894	description: Expert code review specialist. Proactively reviews code for quality, security, and maintainability. Use immediately after writing or modifying code.
895	tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
896	model: inherit
897	---
898	
899	You are a senior code reviewer ensuring high standards of code quality and security.
900	
901	When invoked:
902	1. Run git diff to see recent changes
903	2. Focus on modified files
904	3. Begin review immediately
905	
906	Review checklist:
907	- Code is clear and readable
908	- Functions and variables are well-named
909	- No duplicated code
910	- Proper error handling
911	- No exposed secrets or API keys
912	- Input validation implemented
913	- Good test coverage
914	- Performance considerations addressed
915	
916	Provide feedback organized by priority:
917	- Critical issues (must fix)
918	- Warnings (should fix)
919	- Suggestions (consider improving)
920	
921	Include specific examples of how to fix issues.
922	```
923	
924	### Debugger
925	
926	A subagent that can both analyze and fix issues. Unlike the code reviewer, this one includes Edit because fixing bugs requires modifying code. The prompt provides a clear workflow from diagnosis to verification.
927	
928	```markdown theme={null}
929	---
930	name: debugger
931	description: Debugging specialist for errors, test failures, and unexpected behavior. Use proactively when encountering any issues.
932	tools: Read, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob
933	---
934	
935	You are an expert debugger specializing in root cause analysis.
936	
937	When invoked:
938	1. Capture error message and stack trace
939	2. Identify reproduction steps
940	3. Isolate the failure location
941	4. Implement minimal fix
942	5. Verify solution works
943	
944	Debugging process:
945	- Analyze error messages and logs
946	- Check recent code changes
947	- Form and test hypotheses
948	- Add strategic debug logging
949	- Inspect variable states
950	
951	For each issue, provide:
952	- Root cause explanation
953	- Evidence supporting the diagnosis
954	- Specific code fix
955	- Testing approach
956	- Prevention recommendations
957	
958	Focus on fixing the underlying issue, not the symptoms.
959	```
960	
961	### Data scientist
962	
963	A domain-specific subagent for data analysis work. This example shows how to create subagents for specialized workflows outside of typical coding tasks. It explicitly sets `model: sonnet` for more capable analysis.
964	
965	```markdown theme={null}
966	---
967	name: data-scientist
968	description: Data analysis expert for SQL queries, BigQuery operations, and data insights. Use proactively for data analysis tasks and queries.
969	tools: Bash, Read, Write
970	model: sonnet
971	---
972	
973	You are a data scientist specializing in SQL and BigQuery analysis.
974	
975	When invoked:
976	1. Understand the data analysis requirement
977	2. Write efficient SQL queries
978	3. Use BigQuery command line tools (bq) when appropriate
979	4. Analyze and summarize results
980	5. Present findings clearly
981	
982	Key practices:
983	- Write optimized SQL queries with proper filters
984	- Use appropriate aggregations and joins
985	- Include comments explaining complex logic
986	- Format results for readability
987	- Provide data-driven recommendations
988	
989	For each analysis:
990	- Explain the query approach
991	- Document any assumptions
992	- Highlight key findings
993	- Suggest next steps based on data
994	
995	Always ensure queries are efficient and cost-effective.
996	```
997	
998	### Database query validator
999	
1000	A subagent that allows Bash access but validates commands to permit only read-only SQL queries. This example shows how to use `PreToolUse` hooks for conditional validation when you need finer control than the `tools` field provides.
1001	
1002	```markdown theme={null}
1003	---
1004	name: db-reader
1005	description: Execute read-only database queries. Use when analyzing data or generating reports.
1006	tools: Bash
1007	hooks:
1008	  PreToolUse:
1009	    - matcher: "Bash"
1010	      hooks:
1011	        - type: command
1012	          command: "./scripts/validate-readonly-query.sh"
1013	---
1014	
1015	You are a database analyst with read-only access. Execute SELECT queries to answer questions about the data.
1016	
1017	When asked to analyze data:
1018	1. Identify which tables contain the relevant data
1019	2. Write efficient SELECT queries with appropriate filters
1020	3. Present results clearly with context
1021	
1022	You cannot modify data. If asked to INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or modify schema, explain that you only have read access.
1023	```
1024	
1025	Claude Code [passes hook input as JSON](/en/hooks#pretooluse-input) via stdin to hook commands. The validation script reads this JSON, extracts the command being executed, and checks it against a list of SQL write operations. If a write operation is detected, the script [exits with code 2](/en/hooks#exit-code-2-behavior-per-event) to block execution and returns an error message to Claude via stderr.
1026	
1027	Create the validation script anywhere in your project. The path must match the `command` field in your hook configuration:
1028	
1029	```bash theme={null}
1030	#!/bin/bash
1031	# Blocks SQL write operations, allows SELECT queries
1032	
1033	# Read JSON input from stdin
1034	INPUT=$(cat)
1035	
1036	# Extract the command field from tool_input using jq
1037	COMMAND=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
1038	
1039	if [ -z "$COMMAND" ]; then
1040	  exit 0
1041	fi
1042	
1043	# Block write operations (case-insensitive)
1044	if echo "$COMMAND" | grep -iE '\b(INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP|CREATE|ALTER|TRUNCATE|REPLACE|MERGE)\b' > /dev/null; then
1045	  echo "Blocked: Write operations not allowed. Use SELECT queries only." >&2
1046	  exit 2
1047	fi
1048	
1049	exit 0
1050	```
1051	
1052	On macOS and Linux, make the script executable:
1053	
1054	```bash theme={null}
1055	chmod +x ./scripts/validate-readonly-query.sh
1056	```
1057	
1058	On Windows, write the validation script in PowerShell and add `shell: powershell` to the hook entry. See [running hooks in PowerShell](/en/hooks#windows-powershell-tool).
1059	
1060	The hook receives JSON via stdin with the Bash command in `tool_input.command`. Exit code 2 blocks the operation and feeds the error message back to Claude. See [Hooks](/en/hooks#exit-code-output) for details on exit codes and [Hook input](/en/hooks#pretooluse-input) for the complete input schema.
1061	
1062	## Next steps
1063	
1064	Now that you understand subagents, explore these related features:
1065	
1066	* [Distribute subagents with plugins](/en/plugins) to share subagents across teams or projects
1067	* [Run Claude Code programmatically](/en/headless) with the Agent SDK for CI/CD and automation
1068	* [Use MCP servers](/en/mcp) to give subagents access to external tools and data
1069
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-05-22 17:17:50Z)**_

Based on the official documentation:

**Answer: Session restart required. Mid-session reload is not supported.**

From the docs (line 241-242):
> Subagents are loaded at session start. If you add or edit a subagent file directly on disk, restart your session to load it. Subagents created through the `/agents` interface take effect immediately without a restart.

**Key points:**

1. **Session-start-only loading** — Claude Code scans `.claude/agents/` and `~/.claude/agents/` at session initialization only. Editing files on disk mid-session does not trigger a reload.

2. **No in-session reload command** — There is no command to reload agent definitions mid-session.

3. **Requirements for discovery** (line 165-181):
   - Valid YAML frontmatter with required fields: `name` (unique identifier) and `description`
   - Filename doesn't have to match the `name` field
   - File must be in `.claude/agents/` (project scope) or `~/.claude/agents/` (user scope)
   - Files are scanned recursively, subdirectories don't affect registration
   - If two files in the same scope share the same `name`, Claude Code keeps one and discards the other without warning

4. **Alternative: Use `/agents` interface** — Creating agents through the `/agents` command (not manual file edits) takes effect immediately without restart.

**Recommendation:** Restart your session to load the updated agent definitions.
